label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let's think about this question about causation. I need to determine whether the administrative assistant caused the problem that the receptionist had no pens left on her desk. First, let me summarize the situation: - The receptionist keeps her desk stocked with pens. - Only administrative assistants are allowed to take the pens; faculty members are supposed to buy their own. - Administrative assistants do typically take the pens. - Faculty members also take the pens, despite being supposed to buy their own. - The receptionist has reminded the faculty members multiple times. - On Monday morning, one of the administrative assistants (let's call her AA) sees Professor Smith walking past the desk and both take pens. - Later that day, the receptionist has a problem because there are no pens left. - The question is whether the administrative assistant (AA) caused the problem. Let me analyze the causation here. Causation can be tricky. In philosophy and general reasoning, we often distinguish between "actual cause" and "contributory cause" or
